attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old woman sees her GP with a non-painful ulcer on the base of her right foot. This has been present for three months with no resolution despite topical antiseptic creams and daily dressing changes. There was no history of preceding foot trauma. She has a medical history of type 1 diabetes mellitus, treated with basal bolus insulin, with a recent HbA1c of 8%. She also has hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, and had a previous DVT in her right leg two years ago after an ankle operation.

On examination her blood pressure is 130/80mmHg, pulse 112/min and temperature 38.3oC. and A random finger prick BGL is 24 mmols/L and finger prick ketones is 0 mmols/L. There is a 3x3 cm circular ulcer at the plantar aspect of the base of the great toe with loose, white tissue at the floor of the ulcer. There is warmth and redness tracking to the mid foot and pus can be expressed from the ulcer. The wound can be probed to underlying bone. There is also decreased sensation in both feet. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulses are palpable bilaterally.

An X-ray of the right foot showed cortical erosion of the distal phalanx of hallux.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following should be the focus of management?
attributes.explanation:
This woman is suffering from a diabetic foot ulcer and she is clinically unwell with signs and symptoms suggestive of osteomyelitis. The next best step in management involves surgical debridement to directly remove the infected tissues for sepsis control .

  • Patients should also undergo peripheral neuropathy screening given the severity of the ulcer, as the lack of sensation in the distal extremities prevents patients from detecting any trauma or ulcers that develop.

  • Patients should also be encouraged to conduct daily examinations of the feet for any injuries or infections, preventing the development of diabetic ulcers.

Total contact casting is indicated in the management of neuropathic foot ulcers as it reduces the pressure on the wound and promotes healing. Although this patient may have a neuropathic ulcer, as shown in the decreased sensation in both feet, debridement is the more urgent management option that needs to be considered first in an unwell patient.

Compression stockings are indicated in the management of venous ulcers. This type of ulcer usually occurs above the medial malleolus as it is the area of highest venous pressure from venous insufficiency. It is typically a shallow ulcer that is mildly painful and oedematous, unlike the ulcer that the patient above has.

The dressing change frequency is determined by the various characteristics of ulcers, and an increased frequency of dressing change is usually required in ulcers that are infected or have profuse exudate that continuously soaks the dressing. This would improve the wound environment through consistent removal of dead/infected tissue, thus preventing maceration of the surrounding skin and promoting wound healing. However at the patient is unwell, surgical management should be undertaken prior to change in dressing regimen.

Intravenous antibiotics alone can be considered if there is only soft tissue infection and the patient is not systemically unwell. In this case, the patient is unwell with evidence of bony involvement and surgical debridement is the priority.
learningPoints: Diabetes related foot disease can be managed conservatively or surgically
Evaluation of diabetes related foot ulcer requires comprehensive assessment of local and systemic features of infection
references: Chen P, Carville K, Swanson T, Lazzarini PA, Charles J, Cheney J, Prentice J. Australian guideline on wound healing interventions to enhance healing of foot ulcers: part of the 2021 Australian evidence-based guidelines for diabetes-related foot disease. Journal of Foot and Ankle Research. 2022 Dec;15(1):1-24.

Commons RJ, Charles J, Cheney J, Lynar SA, Malone M, Raby E, Australian Diabetes-related Foot Disease Guidelines & Pathways Project. Australian guideline on management of diabetes-related foot infection: part of the 2021 Australian evidence-based guidelines for diabetes-related foot disease. Journal of Foot and Ankle Research. 2022 Jun 9;15(1):47.

Lipsky BA, Senneville É, Abbas ZG, Aragón-Sánchez J, Diggle M, Embil JM, Kono S, Lavery LA, Malone M, van Asten SA, Urbančič-Rovan V, Peters EJG; International Working Group on the Diabetic Foot (IWGDF). Guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of foot infection in persons with diabetes (IWGDF 2019 update). Journal of Diabetes/Metabolism Research and Reviews. 2020 March 36(S1):e3280
